content:
At My Discombobulated Brain, we believe festivals should be spaces where everyone feels safe, supported, and free to enjoy the energy of the event. We’re here to make sure that mental health and well-being are prioritised, safeguarding your guests and providing the support they need to fully embrace your event.
Guidance and advice for organisers, tips and support for attendees, a register of mental-health-concious events.
summary:

We’re here to make sure that mental health and well-being are prioritised, safeguarding your guests and providing the support they need to fully embrace your event.

Privacy Policy Your privacy is important to us. This policy explains how we handle your data in accordance with UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018.
What information we collect We may collect personal information such as your name, contact details, and any information you provide via our forms or services.
How we use your information We use your information to provide our services, respond to enquiries, and improve our website. We do not sell your data.
Your rights You have the right to access, correct, or request deletion of your data. Contact us for any requests or concerns.
Last updated: 15 May 2026

content:
Our Story We are a dedicated mental health charity committed to providing compassionate support, resources, and advocacy for individuals facing mental health challenges. Founded on the belief that mental health matters just as much as physical health, we work tirelessly to break down stigma and create a more supportive world.
You do not have to go through this on your own. Reaching out is a brave first step, and we are here to walk alongside you.
Our Mission Our mission is threefold:
Support We provide accessible resources, information, and connections to professional services for those experiencing mental health challenges. We believe everyone deserves support, regardless of their circumstances.
Education Through workshops, resources, and open conversations, we work to increase awareness and understanding of mental health issues. Knowledge is power, and we’re committed to empowering our community.
Community We foster safe, inclusive spaces where people can share their experiences, find connection, and receive support without judgment. No one should face mental health challenges alone.
Our Values Compassion: We approach every individual with empathy, understanding, and without judgment.
Accessibility: Mental health support should be available to everyone, regardless of background or circumstance.
Evidence-Based: We rely on scientific research and best practices to guide our programs and resources.
Inclusivity: We welcome and support people of all backgrounds, identities, and experiences.
Hope: We believe in recovery, resilience, and the inherent strength within each person.
What We Do Provide free educational resources and information Connect individuals with professional mental health services Offer support groups and peer connection opportunities Advocate for mental health awareness and policy change Share stories of hope and recovery Create accessible, stigma-free spaces for conversation Get Involved Whether you’re seeking support, want to volunteer, or simply want to learn more about mental health, we’re here for you. Together, we can build a more understanding and supportive community.
Need Support? If you or someone you know is struggling with mental health, please don’t hesitate to reach out. Visit our contact page or call your local crisis helpline.
Remember: Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness. You matter, and your mental health matters.
summary:
Our Story

We are a dedicated mental health charity committed to providing compassionate support, resources, and advocacy for individuals facing mental health challenges. Founded on the belief that mental health matters just as much as physical health, we work tirelessly to break down stigma and create a more supportive world.

You do not have to go through this on your own. Reaching out is a brave first step, and we are here to walk alongside you.
title: About Us

content:
Talking about mental health at work remains difficult for many people. Fear of judgement, concerns about job security, and uncertainty about how a manager might respond all play a part. Yet the cost of staying silent, both to individuals and to organisations, is significant.
Why the silence persists Many people have absorbed the message, often unspoken, that the workplace is not an appropriate place to discuss personal struggles. Professionalism, in many settings, has come to mean keeping difficult feelings out of sight. This is changing, but slowly.
The result is that people often manage significant mental health challenges while performing as though everything is fine. That takes a considerable amount of energy, and over time it tends to make things worse rather than better.
The case for opening up When people feel safe enough to be honest about what they are experiencing, several things become possible. Reasonable adjustments can be made before a situation becomes a crisis. Workloads can be redistributed if needed. A person can get support earlier, when it is often more effective.
For organisations, a culture where mental health is talked about openly tends to see lower rates of long-term sickness absence and higher levels of trust and retention.
How to start the conversation There is no single right way, but a few things tend to help:
Choose the right moment. A brief one-to-one, rather than a busy meeting room, is usually more appropriate. If you are speaking to a manager, asking for a private catch-up gives them time to be present.
You do not need to share everything. It is reasonable to say that you have been finding things difficult without giving a full account of what is happening. You might say something like: “I wanted to let you know I’ve been going through a difficult period and it has been affecting me. I wanted to flag it rather than let it build up.”
Know your rights. Under the Equality Act 2010, if a mental health condition has a substantial and long-term effect on your ability to carry out normal daily activities, it may be considered a disability, and your employer has a duty to make reasonable adjustments.
If you manage others The most important thing a manager can do is create the conditions in which someone might feel comfortable having this conversation. That means responding without alarm, listening without immediately trying to fix, and following up.
summary:
Talking about mental health at work remains difficult for many people. Fear of judgement, concerns about job security, and uncertainty about how a manager might respond all play a part. Yet the cost of staying silent, both to individuals and to organisations, is significant.

Why the silence persists

Many people have absorbed the message, often unspoken, that the workplace is not an appropriate place to discuss personal struggles. Professionalism, in many settings, has come to mean keeping difficult feelings out of sight. This is changing, but slowly.
title: Talking About Mental Health at Work

content:
Anxiety is one of the most common mental health experiences, affecting people in many different ways. For some it appears as a persistent hum of worry in the background of daily life. For others it can feel sudden and overwhelming. Neither experience is unusual, and neither means something is fundamentally wrong with you.
What anxiety actually is At its core, anxiety is your body’s threat-response system doing what it was designed to do. When your brain perceives danger, it triggers a cascade of physical and mental changes, including a faster heartbeat, shallow breathing, and heightened alertness. In situations of genuine danger, this is genuinely useful.
The difficulty arises when that same system activates in response to situations that are not life-threatening, such as a difficult conversation, a looming deadline, or an unfamiliar social situation. Your body reacts as if the threat is real, because to your nervous system, it feels real.
Common signs of anxiety Anxiety can show up physically, emotionally, and behaviourally. You might notice:
Tension in your chest, shoulders, or jaw Difficulty sleeping or waking up with a sense of dread Racing or looping thoughts Avoiding situations that feel overwhelming Irritability or difficulty concentrating These experiences vary from person to person, and they can shift over time.
Some approaches that can help There is no single solution that works for everyone, but the following are worth exploring:
Grounding techniques can help interrupt the anxiety spiral by bringing your attention back to the present moment. The 5-4-3-2-1 method, noticing five things you can see, four you can touch, three you can hear, two you can smell, and one you can taste, is one example.
Paced breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system and can reduce the intensity of a physical anxiety response. Breathing in for four counts, holding for four, and out for six is a commonly used pattern.
Talking to someone is not a sign of weakness. Whether that is a trusted friend, a peer support group, or a trained therapist, being heard makes a difference.
summary:
Anxiety is one of the most common mental health experiences, affecting people in many different ways. For some it appears as a persistent hum of worry in the background of daily life. For others it can feel sudden and overwhelming. Neither experience is unusual, and neither means something is fundamentally wrong with you.

What anxiety actually is

At its core, anxiety is your body’s threat-response system doing what it was designed to do. When your brain perceives danger, it triggers a cascade of physical and mental changes, including a faster heartbeat, shallow breathing, and heightened alertness. In situations of genuine danger, this is genuinely useful.
title: Understanding Anxiety: What It Is and How to Cope
